trivia_bullets.text: Masticore is a creature type coined in Urza's Destiny (1999) for metallic lion-like artifact creatures — the name derives from 'masticate' (to chew). The original Masticore was a format-defining card in Standard-era Urza block. Sparkhunter Masticore is the fifth named variant in the lineage, following Masticore (Urza's Destiny), Razormane Masticore (Fifth Dawn), Molten-Tail Masticore (Scars of Mirrodin), and Lesser Masticore (Modern Horizons). All Masticores require a discard as part of their casting or upkeep cost — a design signature of the type. [MTG Wiki 'Masticore' page, via WebSearch; Scryfall]

Sparkhunter Masticore is the first Masticore designed as explicit planeswalker hate: it has protection from planeswalkers, can deal 1 damage to any planeswalker for 1 mana, and can gain indestructible for 3 mana. As a colorless 3/4 for three mana (with a discard cost), it fits into any deck as a sideboard piece against planeswalker-heavy strategies. Per coverage at Dot Esports (June 2020) and Star City Games, it was considered a solid sideboard card across nearly every archetype except aggressive decks where discarding a card mid-game is too steep a tempo loss. EDHREC rank of 24,516 indicates minimal Commander traction — in Commander, planeswalkers are less dominant, reducing the hate's value. [Scryfall; Dot Esports 'M21 spoiler Sparkhunter Masticore marks MTG planeswalkers as the enemy,' via WebSearch; SCG via WebSearch]

Sparkhunter Masticore is a first printing — no reprints as of M21's release — introduced specifically for the M21 Standard environment in which Teferi, Time Raveler and other planeswalkers were defining the format. The card previewed via a Wizards of the Coast Twitch stream on June 5, 2020. Art by Randy Vargas — a prolific MTG artist whose work frequently depicts artifact creatures in dramatic environmental settings. [Scryfall; Scryfall preview source field]

The discard-to-cast clause is the defining design constraint on all Masticores — a callback to the original 1999 card's upkeep discard. For Sparkhunter, discarding a card at cast-time means it can never be a turn-one or top-decked-from-an-empty-hand play; it requires the player to have a surplus card in hand, gating it behind a real resource cost. This makes it a sideboard piece rather than a main-deck stable even when planeswalkers are dominant. [Scryfall; MTG Wiki 'Masticore' via WebSearch]
